mission.title: Install and Configure Copa on Kubernetes
mission.description: Copa is a CLI tool that allows you to directly patch container images without the need for full rebuilds, addressing critical security vulnerabilities quickly. Installing Copa enables you to enhance your container security posture by applying patches efficiently.

steps.title: Add the Copa Helm repository
steps.description:
First, add the Copa Helm repository to your local Helm setup with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add copa https://project-copacetic.github.io/copacetic/
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Copa using Helm
steps.description:
Now, install Copa in a dedicated namespace (e.g., copa) with the following command:
```bash
helm install copa copa/copa --namespace copa --create-namespace --version v0.13.0
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the Copa pod is running successfully by executing:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace copa
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
To ensure Copa runs efficiently, you can set resource limits by editing the deployment:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment copa --namespace copa
``` 
Add the following under `spec.containers.resources`:
```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    memory: "512Mi"
    cpu: "500m"
```

steps.title: Test Copa functionality
steps.description:
You can test Copa by running a simple command to patch an image. Replace `<image>` with the actual image you want to patch:
```bash
kubectl run test-copa --image=<image> --namespace copa --command -- copa patch <image>
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Copa will show the Copa pod running in the specified namespace. You can also verify its functionality by executing a patch command on a container image.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add copa https://project-copacetic.github.io/copacetic/
helm repo update
helm install copa copa/copa --namespace copa --create-namespace --version v0.13.0

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
To cleanly remove the Copa installation, first uninstall the Helm release with the following command:
```bash
helm uninstall copa --namespace copa
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Next, you should remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent resources that were created. Run the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete crd copas.copa.io
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace copa
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Finally, delete the namespace used for Copa and verify its removal:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace copa
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's important to back up the current state of your installation. You can do this by exporting the current configuration:
```bash
helm get values copa --namespace copa > copa-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Next, update the Helm repository and run the upgrade command to update Copa to the latest version:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade copa copa/copa --namespace copa --version v0.14.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After the upgrade, verify that the Copa pod is running the new version successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace copa
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the Copa pod is stuck in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for errors:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace copa
```
Look for any error messages that indicate what might be going wrong.

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails with version conflict
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter a version conflict during the Helm upgrade, ensure that the version you are trying to upgrade to is compatible. Check the installed version:
```bash
helm list --namespace copa
```
Then, specify a compatible version in your upgrade command.

troubleshooting.title: Resource limits causing OOMKilled
troubleshooting.description:
If the Copa pod is being terminated due to OOMKilled, consider increasing the memory limit in the deployment:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment copa --namespace copa
```
Update the `resources.limits.memory` value to a higher amount.

troubleshooting.title: Unable to connect to the Copa service
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot connect to the Copa service, check the service status and endpoints:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace copa
kubectl describe svc copa --namespace copa
```
Ensure that the service is correctly configured and that the endpoints are available.

prerequisites.description: You need a Kubernetes cluster and Helm installed on your local machine.
